visual_checks: Check whether the page content is divided into logical sections with visible headings
Look for long blocks of content that lack any headings to break them into sections
Verify that heading levels reflect the document structure (h1 for page title, h2 for sections, h3 for subsections)
Check if form groups, navigation areas, and content regions have descriptive headings
Look for content that uses bold or large text to visually simulate headings without using heading elements
Verify that every distinct topic or section of content begins with an appropriate heading
pass_conditions: All content sections are introduced with descriptive heading elements (h1–h6)
Heading hierarchy is logical and reflects the content structure without skipping levels
Long-form content is broken into sections with headings that describe each section's topic
Form sections and fieldsets are organized under headings that describe the group
Navigation regions and other landmark areas include headings where appropriate
fail_conditions: Content is presented as a long block of text with no section headings to organize it
Visual headings are created with bold or large text but not marked up as heading elements
Heading elements are used but they do not logically organize the content into sections
Major content sections exist without any heading to introduce them
Headings are present but skip levels in a way that misrepresents the content structure
na_conditions: Page content is so brief that it consists of a single short section requiring no subdivision
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Screenshots could not be captured for visual verification
Page content could not be fully loaded to evaluate section organization
common_mistakes: Using div or span with CSS styling to create visual headings instead of proper heading elements
Having only an h1 with no sub-headings even when the content covers multiple distinct topics
Skipping heading levels (e.g., h1 directly to h4) which misrepresents the document outline
Using headings for styling purposes rather than to organize content sections
Lengthy FAQ or policy pages where questions are styled as bold text rather than heading elements

examples.pass: A company About page uses h1 for the page title, h2 headings for 'Our Mission', 'Our Team', 'Our History', and h3 headings for team department subsections — all content is logically organized under section headings
examples.fail: A terms of service page presents 15 paragraphs of content with only an h1 page title and no section headings — users cannot navigate to specific sections or understand the content structure
examples.partial: Most page sections have proper headings but the FAQ section uses bold paragraph text for questions instead of heading elements, making those sections inaccessible to heading navigation

plain_meaning: When content is organized into sections, section headings should be used to introduce each section. This helps users navigate and understand the structure. It is a AAA requirement and applies where content is genuinely organized into sections that warrant headings.
scope_applies_to: Long-form content divided into topical sections
Articles, documents, and pages with distinct content blocks
Any place where sectioning aids navigation and comprehension
Use of heading elements or role=heading to mark section starts
scope_does_not_apply_to: Short pages with a single topic and no meaningful sub-sections
Whether headings are descriptive (that is SC 2.4.6)
Whether heading levels form a correct hierarchy (that is SC 1.3.1)
borderline_calibration: Only flag when the content is clearly organized into sections that lack any heading.
A single-topic page or a pure landing page does not require section headings.
Do not demand arbitrary subdivision of short content.
The SC says section headings are 'used to organize' content, not that every visual block must have a heading.
